title: SFAT Application No. 2/2021
text:
Application No. 2 of 2021
IN THE SECURITIES AND FUTURES APPEALS TRIBUNAL
______________________________________
IN THE MATTER OF the Decisions made by the
Securities and Futures Commission under
section 194 of the Securities and Futures Ordinance,
Cap. 571
AND IN THE MATTER OF section 217 of the
Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap. 571
______________________________________
BETWEEN
CARDINALASIA CONSULTING LIMITED 1st Applicant
LEE SHIU LUN EDWARD 2nd Applicant
And
SECURITIES AND FUTURES COMMISSION Respondent
_________________________________
Tribunal: Mr Michael Hartmann, Chairman
Dates of Hearing: 7, 8, 10, 11, 17 March & 6 April 2022
Date of Determination: 27 January 2023
____________________
DETERMINATION
________________________

A A
The Applicants
B B
1. This application for review has been brought by two Applicants (‘the
C C
Applicants’). The first is a company registered in Hong Kong, Cardinalasia
Consulting Limited (‘Cardinalasia’). The second is the sole shareholder of that
D D
company, Lee Shiu Lun (‘Edward Lee’), a man of high academic qualification and
E
experience in matters of finance.
E
F F
2. At all times relevant to this matter, that is, between about August
2014 and October 2017, the Applicants, that is, both Cardinalasia and Edward Lee,
G G
were licensed by the Securities and Futures Commission (‘the SFC’) to carry on
H H
Type 4 (advising on securities) and Type 9 (asset management) regulated activities
pursuant to the provisions of the Securities and Futures Ordinance, Cap 571 (‘the
I I
Ordinance’). As licensed persons, both Applicants were subject to the Code of
J Conduct1. J
K K
3. Edward Lee, the Second Applicant, was further approved to act as
the Responsible Officer of Cardinalasia. In respect of all matters relevant to this
L L
application for review, it is accepted that Edward Lee has played a primary and
M decisive role in representing Cardinalasia. M
N N
An introductory overview
O O
4. In early 2014, in Hong Kong, the Second Applicant, Edward Lee,
P struck up a business relationship with a man named Yeung Chun Wai (‘Anthony P
Yeung’), also a man of experience in the financial sector. Anthony Yeung intended
Q Q
to set up and run a number of investment funds and Edward Lee agreed to assist him
in this undertaking.
R R
S S
T T
1 That is: the “Code of Conduct for Persons licensed by or registered with the Securities and Futures
Commission”.
U U
- 2 -

A A
5. Although no documentary evidence to that effect was put before the
B Tribunal, it would appear that Anthony Yeung was ‘the promoter’ of the investment B
funds in the sense that he appears to have been primarily responsible for setting up
C C
the funds.
D D
6. Between about April 2014 and June 2015, five investment funds
E
were set up, each fund investing in small and medium enterprise (‘SME’) stocks.
E
F F
7. The offering memorandum given to potential investors by each fund
made it clear that the investment strategy of each would be aggressive in nature,
G G
seeking returns of 20% per annum. To achieve such returns, potential investors were
H H
informed that each fund would employ leverage mechanisms in order to enhance
returns, the limit being around 300% of each fund’s net asset value. That being the
I I
case, it followed that margin financing would be used2.
J J
8. Potential investors were further informed that, with each fund
K K
concentrating on SME stocks, there would be a limited diversification of investment
which itself “may expose the Fund to losses disproportionate to those incurred by
L L
the market in general”.
M M
9. The five funds that were set up – the ‘Funds’ – were:
N N
O (1) the Taiping Quantum China Opportunities Fund launched in April O
2014 (the ‘Opportunities Fund’);
P P
(2) the Taiping Quantum Strategic Fund launched in May 2014 (the
‘Strategic Fund’);
Q Q
(3) the Taiping Quantum Prosperity Fund launched in July 2014 (the
R R
‘Prosperity Fund’);
S S
2 As to the intention of the Funds to borrow money, potential investors were informed that: “The Fund will
T T
employ leverage including, without limitation, through pledge assets as security for borrowings for
enhancement of return. The limit of the leverage is around 300% of the Fund’s net asset value.”
U U
- 3 -

A A
(4) the Quantum Advantage Fund launched in March 2015 (the
B B
Advantage Fund’) and
(5) the Quantum Enhanced Fund launched in June 2015 (the ‘Enhanced
C C
Fund’).
D D
10. Between about August 2014 and October 2017, Cardinalasia, under
E the control of Edward Lee, acted as Principal Investment Adviser (‘Investment E
Adviser’) to these five Funds, each appointment being in terms of an investment
F F
advisory agreement.
G G
11. In December 2020, after a period of investigation, the Securities and
H
Futures Commission (‘the SFC’) informed the two Applicants that it had come to
H
the preliminary determination that, during the time that Cardinalasia, under the
I I
control of Edward Lee, had acted as Investment Adviser to the Funds, the company
J and Edward Lee, as the company’s Responsible Officer, had failed, as licensed J
persons under the Ordinance, to give appropriate advice to the Manager of each
K K
Fund in an attempt to ensure fair dealing in respect of two matters that were at the
time of importance to the financial integrity of the Funds.
L L
M 12. Those matters were, first, a series of loan arrangements entered into M
between the funds at a time when certain of the Funds were experiencing severe
N N
liquidity problems and, second, a little later in time, a series of cross trades entered
O into between the Funds at a time when the Managers of the Funds were seeking a O
rebalancing of their investment portfolios.
P P
13. It was the SFC assertion that the Applicants, in their role as
Q Q
Investment Advisers, had failed to seek to protect the best interests of the Funds in
R respect of the internal loan arrangements and the cross trades and that, in the wake R
of the Applicants’ asserted neglect, certain of the Funds had been prejudiced
S S
financially.
T T
U U
- 4 -

A A
14. In light of these asserted failures, it was the SFC’s preliminary
B finding that the Applicants, as licensed persons, had acted in a culpable manner B
under the Code of Conduct in that they had failed to -
C C
D (a) ensure that the ‘internal loan arrangements’ entered into between the D
Funds had been fair to, and in the best interests of, both the
E E
borrowing and lending Funds, and
F (b) had further failed to properly assess the basis for certain of the cross F
trades conducted between the funds in order to ensure that they had
G G
been executed on the best available terms for, and in the best interests
of, both the buying and selling funds.
H H
I
15. It was further said that Edward Lee, as Responsible Officer for
I
Cardinalasia, in failing to ensure the maintenance of appropriate standards of
J J
conduct, that is, conduct seeking to bring about fair outcomes for investors in the
K funds, had been in breach of General Principle 9 of the Code of Conduct which K
relates to the responsibilities of senior management. General Principle 9 commences
L L
by stating that -
M M
“The senior management of a licensed or registered person should bear
primary responsibility for ensuring the maintenance of appropriate
N N
standards of conduct and adherence to proper procedures…”
O O
16. As to the imposition of sanctions, the SFC informed the Applicants
P that it proposed the following – P
Q Q
(a) that Cardinalasia, the company, be publicly reprimanded and fined a
sum of HK$1,500,000;
R R
(b) that Edward Lee’s licence to act as a representative in respect of all
S S
regulated activities be suspended for a period of seven months.
T T
U U
- 5 -

A A
17. Both Applicants, through their legal representative, sought - without
B success - to persuade the SFC that its preliminary determinations of culpability were B
without merit. In the result, in decision notices dated 19 July 2021, the Applicants
C C
were informed that the SFC had come to final determinations confirming their
D culpability and that the sanctions earlier proposed were now confirmed. D
E
18. In August 2021, the Applicants filed their Notice of Application for
E
Review with the Tribunal, seeking to set aside the SFC findings of culpability in
F F
respect of both the internal loan arrangements and the cross trades. An amended
G version of that notice was filed with the Tribunal in October 2021. G
H H
19. It was further the case for the Applicants that, should they not be
successful in having the findings of culpability set aside, they sought nevertheless
I I
to challenge the reasonableness of the sanctions imposed upon them.
J J
The regulatory requirement for the investment advisory agreements
K K
20. It is apparent that a material reason why Cardinalasia was chosen as
L L
Investment Adviser to each Fund lay in the fact that it - and Edward Lee – were
M licensed under the Ordinance to carry on Type 4, and especially Type 9, regulated M
activities.
N N
21. At no time between 2014 and 2017 did the companies which
O O
managed each of the Funds hold a Type 9 licence and accordingly it was accepted
P by Edward Lee that in the circumstances it was a regulatory requirement that each P
Fund enter into an investor advisory agreement.
Q Q
22. In order to put matters into context, it should also be said that the
R R
offering memorandum given to potential investors - under the heading of ‘risk
S management’ - spoke of each Fund placing reliance on its management team, this S
team including each Manager and each Investment Adviser:
T T
U U
- 6 -

A A
“Although the Directors have the ultimate authority and responsibility for the
management of the Fund, subject to the parameters determined by the Directors,
B B
the decisions relating to the investment of the Fund’s assets have been delegated
to the Manager which in turn are delegated to the Principal Investment Adviser…”
C C
23. The offering memorandum continued by stressing the fact that the
D D
loss of the Investment Adviser “could materially and negatively impact the value of
the fund.”
E E
F 24. At this early stage of the determination, it is important to point to the F
fact that Edward Lee, the Second Applicant, in the course of his testimony, spoke
G G
of the range of his duties being mapped out not entirely in accordance with the
written terms and conditions of the investment advisory agreements but instead, to
H H
a greater and more decisive extent, being mapped out in accordance with private,
I I
unwritten arrangements reached with Anthony Yeung.
J J
25. It was Edward Lee’s evidence that he considered that the unwritten
K arrangements - seemingly never formalised in any way and never passed on to third K
parties (such as the SFC) - were determinative of the nature and extent of his
L L
obligations. Accordingly, when referred to the citation set out above (in paragraph
22), this being information supplied to potential investors in each Fund, he stated
M M
that this was inaccurate and did not reflect the role of Cardinalasia, the First
N Applicant, in the management of the Funds. However, this inaccuracy in an N
important set of documents was not something which he referred to Anthony Yeung.
O O
26. It was the effect of Edward Lee’s own case therefore that potential
P P
investors reading offering memoranda would have been given incorrect information
Q Q
as to the true role of the Investment Adviser.
R R
The Code of Conduct
S S
27. Of course, as licensed persons under the Ordinance, both Applicants
T were subject to the Code of Conduct, it being a fundamental requirement of the Code T
U U
- 7 -

A A
that licensed persons must deal with each client - each being considered separately
B - in a manner that is fair. In this regard - B
C C
(a) The first general principle of the Code provides that, in conducting
D their licensed business activities, licensed persons (such as the D
Applicants) must act honestly, fairly, in the best interests of their
E E
clients and in accordance with the integrity of the market.
F (b) A complementary principle requires licensed persons to ensure that F
client positions in the market or assets held are adequately
G G
safeguarded.
H (c) The Code further makes reference to conflicts of interest – of direct H
relevance in the present matter - requiring that licensed persons
I I
must seek to avoid such conflicts but, if they cannot be avoided,
they must ensure that their clients are fairly treated.
J J
K The hearing before this Tribunal K
L L
28. Prior to the hearing, the parties agreed in writing, in accordance with
the provisions of the Ordinance, that the Chairman should determine the application
M M
for review alone.
N N
29. The substantive hearing was held over a period of five days in March
O O
2022 with final submissions being made on a day in April 2022. Due to the fact that
P the Chairman was at the time in Australia and was prevented, by reason of prevailing P
Hong Kong Covid 19 regulations, from returning to Hong Kong, the full hearing
Q Q
was conducted by way of video communication: the parties and counsel being in
Hong Kong, the Chairman in Australia.
R R
S S
T T
U U
- 8 -

A A
Underlying legal principles
B B
30. Before looking to the disputed issues before this Tribunal, reference
C C
must be made to certain of the fundamental legal principles that have governed the
context in which the Tribunal has reached its determination of the merits.
D D
E A. This application constitutes a ‘de novo’ hearing E
F F
31. In determining an application for review, it is now well established
that the Tribunal conducts a hearing de novo, undertaking a full merits review. Its
G G
duty, therefore, is not to act as a court of appeal but rather to act as a court of first
H instance. The duty to conduct a hearing de novo extends also to matters of possible H
sanctions.
I I
32. On the basis that this application is conducted as a hearing de novo,
J J
it follows that the burden of proof has at all times remained on the SFC.
K K
B. The standard of proof
L L
33. Section 218 (7) of the Ordinance directs that the standard of proof
M M
required to determine any issue in this application is the standard applicable to civil
N proceedings. Matters must therefore be proved on a balance of probabilities. This N
standard requires that the Tribunal be satisfied that an event has occurred if it
O O
considers that, on the evidence, the occurrence of the event was more likely than
not.
P P
Q C. Expert evidence Q
R R
34. During the course of the hearing, the Tribunal received evidence
which, after consideration, it accepted as being expert evidence. That evidence was
S S
given by Richard Harris. The Tribunal received that evidence because it was likely
T to be outside the knowledge and experience of the Tribunal. That being said, the T
Tribunal recognised that it was entitled to accept or reject all or part of that evidence,
U U
- 9 -

A A
coming to its own conclusion on such matters based on a consideration of all the
B evidence. B
C C
35. As it is, in respect of the expert evidence of Richard Harris, the
Tribunal found it to be balanced, prudent and based on years of experience working
D D
in different financial jurisdictions. As it was put by counsel for the SFC in final
E
submissions: Mr Harris had substantial experience in fund management, stock
E
brokerage and even private banking roles, with exposure to both global and Asian
F F
securities and small-cap stocks in particular. The Tribunal found Mr Harris to be an
G impressive witness, his testimony, as an objective observer, being both balanced and G
considered.
H H
D. Good character
I I
36. During the course of the hearing, it was emphasised that Edward Lee,
J J
the Second Applicant, held academic qualifications of the highest order and, arising
K K
from this, was able to boast an excellent professional record. He was a man therefore
who could be expected to have a sophisticated understanding of how best to protect
L L
the interests of clients in adverse times even if what he did was not entirely orthodox.
M The Tribunal has taken this into account. M
N N
37. It was also stressed that Edward Lee was a man of good character in
that he had not at any earlier time been found guilty of any criminal offences or
O O
regulatory infraction. The Tribunal has taken this into account, doing so in the
P
following manner, namely, that a person of good character such as Edward Lee is
P
less likely than otherwise might be the case to have committed the misconduct
Q Q
alleged and, in addition, good character supports his credibility in respect of both
R his evidence before the Tribunal and his evidence provided in his records of R
interview and other statements.
S S
T T
U U
- 10 -

A A
The fact that Anthony Yeung did not give evidence before the Tribunal
B B
38. It should be said that, although Anthony Yeung would have been a
C C
central character in both the internal loan arrangements and the cross trades, he did
not give evidence before the Tribunal. In this regard, in directions filed prior to the
D D
hearing, it was agreed that certain documents may be admitted into evidence without
E
the need to call their makers. These documents included records of Anthony
E
Yeung’s interviews with the SFC.
F F
The management structure of the five Funds
G G
H 39. An understanding of the management structure of each of the Funds H
is fundamental in this matter.
I I
40. In the view of the Tribunal, in looking to that management structure,
J J
an important point of emphasis is that, although the Funds were each concentrated
K on SME investments, although each shared aggressive investment policies, and K
although the managing personalities were the same, each Fund was structured as an
L L
independent corporate structure and no doubt that is how they would have been seen
M by investors. M
N 41. In this regard, each Fund was incorporated as a Cayman Islands N
exempted company limited by shares. As an incorporated body, each fund had its
O O
own board of directors, the board being the ultimate source of authority with power
P to direct and supervise all investment and managerial matters concerning that Fund. P
Anthony Yeung sat on the board of directors of each Fund, he and two others.
Q Q
42. Each board of directors appointed a ‘Manager’ to be responsible for
R R
providing investment management and advisory services, effectively in charge of –
S that is, the CEO – of each Fund, and in this regard, initially, each Fund appointed S
Quantum China Asset Management Limited (‘QCAML’) a company incorporated
T T
in the Cayman Islands, as its Manager.
U U
- 11 -

A A
43. QCAML’s sole shareholder was Anthony Yeung who, together with
B two others, sat on the board of directors of QCAML. B
C C
44. As to the duties of QCAML, it was set down in each management
agreement that –
D D
E “… During the term of this agreement, the Manager shall provide E
investment management and advisory services to the Fund in accordance
with the investment objectives and policies determined by the Fund’s
F F
directors and subject to the investment restrictions set by the Funds
directors and described in the offering memorandum. In particular, the
Manager shall –
G G
(a) prepare and make available for use at the request of the Fund such
H research reports, trading advice, and such other advisory services H
that the Manager provides to its internal and external clients;
I I
(b) review and evaluate the proposed asset acquisition and investment
strategies of the Fund when required to do so by the Fund or as may
J be necessary from time to time; J
(c) execute, or cause to be executed, purchases and sales of investments
K K
on behalf of the fund as the Manager, in its discretion and subject to
the parameters determined by the Fund’s directors, deemed to be in
L the best interests of the Fund; L
(d) carry out reviews of the investments whenever the Manager shall
M
deem it necessary, or when required to do so by the Fund;
M
(e) recommend to the Fund and the Administrator the manner in which
N N
monies required for the redemption or repurchase of participating
shares or for other purposes of the fund should be realized;
O O
(f) advise the fund and the Administrator as to the making of
distributions by the fund;
P P
(g) open any correspondence it receives that is addressed to the Fund
Q and to promptly notify the directors of the Fund of any matter Q
requiring their attention;
R R
(h) prepare material for inclusion in the annual and other financial
reports of the Fund whenever the Fund shall properly require such
S material; S
(i) enter into side letters for an on behalf of the Fund with potential
T T
investors and investors of the Fund, and
U U
- 12 -

A A
(j) issue the annual audited financial statement of the Fund to
shareholders at their registered addresses within six months of the
B B
financial year end.”
C C
45. These specific obligations, however, did not limit the broad
D discretionary authority given to the Manager to trade in shares, this authority being D
described in the following terms –
E E
“Subject to the investment objectives and policies and investment
F F
restrictions as set out in the offering memorandum, to the overall
supervision of the directors of the Fund and to the directions given by the
board of directors of the Fund, the Manager shall have complete discretion
G G
in the investment and reinvestment of the investments with full power and
authority to make purchases and sales of investments or to issue directly
H to a broker or dealer orders for such purchases and sales of investments.” H
I I
46. Of importance in this matter, in accordance with each agreement, the
Manager had the power to delegate its powers, that power of delegation being broad
J J
in its terms and being described as follows:
K K
“The Manager may appoint delegates to perform in whole or part any of
L its duties or obligations upon such terms as to authority, liability and L
indemnity as shall be determined by the Manager and subject to the
parameters determined by the Fund’s directors. The Manager shall
M M
exercise due care and diligence in such appointment and shall supervise
the conduct of such delegates.”3 [emphasis added]
N N
47. On the evidence, it was pursuant to these delegatory powers that, in
O O
a series of separate investment advisory agreements, QCAML appointed
P Cardinalasia to be the Principal Investment Adviser – the ‘Investment Adviser’ - to P
each of the Funds. It is, in this regard, of significance to note that the responsibilities
Q Q
assumed by the Investment Adviser in terms of each of the investment advisory
R agreements were effectively a direct substitution of the responsibilities assumed by R
the Manager in each management agreement.
S S
T T
3 This citation and the ones set out earlier are taken from the agreement dated 3 August 2016 between the
Enhanced fund and QCAML.
U U
- 13 -

A A
48. In short, in terms of each agreement, while the Manager, of course,
B retained important powers of supervision over the Investment Adviser, and therefore B
retained the authority, if required, to disagree in whole or part with any advice
C C
received, or to set aside any actions taken, the Manager nevertheless, for all effective
D purposes, delegated ‘in whole’ its duties and obligations to the Investment Adviser. D
In this regard, the duties of the Investment Adviser detailed in each contract were
E E
described in the exact same language as the duties of the Manager under each
management agreement. By way of illustration –
F F
G “… during the term of this agreement, the principal investment adviser G
shall provide investment management and advisory services to the
Manager in respect of the Fund in accordance with the investment
H H
objectives and policies determined by the Fund’s directors and subject to
the investment restrictions set by the Fund’s directors and described in the
offering memorandum.”
I I
J 49. That, when delegated, the Investment Adviser’s duties were (for all J
intents and purposes) a duplication of the Manager’s broad duties was further
K K
explained in offering memoranda given to potential investors, it being stated that
L the Investment Adviser would – L
M
“… be responsible for providing investment management and advisory
M
services to the Manager in respect of the Fund in accordance with the
Fund’s investment objectives and policies; providing reasonable assistance
N N
to the Manager in the preparation of offering materials, financial records,
reports to investors and regulatory filings; evaluating the proposed asset
O acquisition and investment strategies of the Fund; executing (or causing to O
be executed) purchases and sales of investments on behalf of the Fund;
recommending the manner in which monies required for the redemption
P P
or repurchase of participating shares or for purposes of the Fund should be
realised; and advising as to the making of distributions by the Fund.”
Q Q
50. On any ordinary reading, it is apparent that the delegated duties of
R R
the Investment Adviser were not limited to proffering advice concerning what
S stocks to buy, hold or sell. The duties of the Investment Adviser, acting in its S
delegated role, were more diverse, looking, by way of ‘investment management’
T T
and by way of providing ‘advisory services’, to protecting the general interests of
U U
- 14 -

A A
each Fund, each Fund, of course, being an investment fund. By way of illustration,
B the duties included the following – B
C C
(a) to review and evaluate the proposed asset acquisition and investment
D strategies of the Fund when required to do so by the Manager or as D
may be necessary …
E E
(b) to recommend to the Manager and the Administrator the manner in
F which monies required for the redemption or repurchase of F
participating shares or for other purposes of the Fund should be
G G
utilised…
H H
51. On the basis of these provisions, the Investment Adviser would have
had an important advisory role to play concerning proposed asset acquisitions and,
I I
in addition, would have had a complementary role to play by way of giving advice
J concerning the use of monies required for “other purposes4. J
K K
Edward Lee’s evidence as to an overriding ‘understanding’.
L L
52. As the Tribunal understands it, it was Edward Lee’s evidence, set out
M in his witness statement, that there was an overriding understanding constituting an M
agreement reached between himself and Anthony Yeung in terms of which his role
N N
as Investment Adviser was far more limited than the role set out in the formal
agreements, those formal agreements being described by Edward Lee as being just
O O
‘legal template’. This agreement, he said, was reached at a coffee shop meeting in
P P
February 2014.
Q Q
53. In terms of this verbal agreement, Cardinalasia’s responsibilities
R would be restricted to three matters. These matters were, first, advising on R
investments but only when required by the directors of each Manager Company or
S S
T T
4 By way of illustration, within the present context, it would seem to the Tribunal that advice concerning
the use of monies for purposes other than buying investments would include making loans
U U
- 15 -

A A
the board of directors of each Fund; second, facilitating the operations of the Funds,
B more of an administrative back-up role, and third, placing orders with stockbrokers B
when directed by the Manager of one of the Funds. However, when he testified
C C
before the Tribunal, these ‘specifics’ appeared to be reduced to what may – at best
D - be understood as ‘general understandings’. In this regard, the following appears D
from the record of cross examination –
E E
Q. Mr Lee, I will ask you one more time. Mr Yeung, at this meeting in
F February 2014, did not specifically tell you what your duties or F
responsibilities may be as Investment Adviser?
G G
A. Well, at that time I cannot remember. But Mr. Yeung is well experienced
and I am also well experienced. So it is a mutual understanding, it is a
H normal understanding that the back-support work will be conducted by us. H
Q. He did not tell you that you were only required to advise on investments
I I
when required by the Fund Manager or board of directors to do so?
J A. Well, I cannot remember whether he had mentioned or not. But it is they J
who will invest with the money they raised.
K K
54. In short, when questioned, it was the essential ambit of Edward Lee’s
L L
answers that, as both Anthony Yeung and himself were experienced financiers, and
as Anthony Yeung had many years of experience in running funds, Anthony Yeung
M M
would require him to play a back-up role only. In this regard, Edward Lee said:
N N
“Well, at that time I cannot remember. But Mr Yeung is well experienced
O and I am also well experienced so it is a mutual understanding, it is a O
normal understanding that the back-support work will be conducted by us.”
P P
55. Much earlier in the SFC proceedings, however, Edward Lee appears
Q Q
to have denied any settled verbal agreement being reached in February 2014. At the
time when investigations were being made, Edward Lee had informed the SFC that
R R
no specific, clearly demarcated role had been given to him at that early stage. As it
S was put: “It was not said very solidly… what would be advised on.” S
T T
U U
- 16 -

A A
56. In the view of the Tribunal, concerning the coffee shop meeting in
B February 2014, taking into account that it was a very early meeting, the Tribunal is B
satisfied that Edward Lee’s statement to the SFC would have been far more accurate.
C C
Detailed agreement as to responsibilities would have come later and would have
D included the drafting of the investment advisory agreements. D
E
57. That later stage - manifestly – would have been the time to give
E
instructions to the draftsman as to the intended scope of duties of the Investment
F F
Adviser to each Fund and thereafter, when the drafts were ready, to discuss their
G intent and scope with involved parties. G
H H
58. No doubt the agreements that were signed – the investment advisory
agreements - constituted a form of ‘legal template’, that is, very much a form of
I I
standard terms and conditions relative to the matter at hand. But it is to be
J remembered that such agreements had a regulatory basis and were required, J
therefore, to be detailed, setting out clearly all respective obligations so that they
K K
could be understood by interested parties (including investors and potential
L investors) and would constitute an important basis for the protection of their rights. L
M 59. During the course of the hearing, at least as the Tribunal understood M
it, considerable emphasis was placed on Edward Lee’s assertion that his true
N N
responsibilities under the Code of Conduct were to be considered within the context
O of the private verbal arrangements reached with Anthony Yeung and not within the O
context of the formal agreements entered into, that is, the investment advisory
P P
agreements. In short, it was submitted that the formal agreements which were never
formally altered, and which would have constituted protection for investors and
Q Q
